docs.title: Papers
docs.text:
1. Agentic Strategies in a Six-Agent Operations Fleet — experience report on patterns and anti-patterns.
  2. Failure Modes in Long-Running Agent Collaboration — deeper incident experience report.
  3. A Role-Oriented Taxonomy for Multi-Agent Systems — framework paper.
  4. Memory-Centric Collaboration — position paper on shared state and orchestration.

docs.title: Pattern examples supplement
docs.text: The pattern catalogue includes one short example per pattern and anti-pattern. The original proposal that led to that decision is retained as a historical note: pattern examples supplement.

docs.title: Publishing charter
docs.text: All papers and artefacts use anonymised, generalised examples drawn from operational experience. No product names, internal identifiers, hostnames, code paths, or infrastructure details appear in the public material. See the publishing charter.

docs.title: Publishing Charter
docs.text: The public research material in this directory is intended for external publication as a set of papers and a mini-site. It must contain no Eden-specific implementation details, intellectual property, or internal identifiers.

docs.title: Public naming conventions
docs.text:
• Do not use the name Eden in public papers or site copy.
  • Use stylised role names for agents: Dispatcher, Builder, Verifier, Researcher, Runtime, Archivist.
  • Refer to the system generically as a multi-agent operations fleet or agent fleet.

docs.title: Incident archetypes
docs.text:
Concrete incidents from internal operations should be transformed into incident archetypes:

  • Remove dates, hostnames, and project names.
  • Replace specific failures with their operational category.
  • Keep only the interaction pattern, root-cause category, and lesson.

Example:

  • Internal: On 3 November a registry build failed because a clean step removed the lockfile and the CI runner cache was stale.
  • Public archetype: A build agent invalidated its own dependency lockfile while the runner image cache was stale, causing a non-deterministic build failure.

docs.title: Acceptable public content
docs.text:
• Roles and interaction patterns
  • Failure-mode taxonomies
  • Design principles and trade-offs
  • Evaluation methods and metrics
  • High-level architecture patterns (without Eden-specific wiring)

docs.title: Prohibited public content
docs.text:
• Product name, logo, or branding
  • Source code, schemas, or API signatures
  • Internal hostnames, IPs, cloud accounts, or deployment details
  • Private conversations or identifiable user messages
  • Commercial plans, pricing, or unreleased features

docs.title: Review process
docs.text:
Before any public document is published:

  1. Author self-checks against this charter.
  2. A second reviewer confirms no Eden-specific material remains.
  3. Final approval by Yakov Khalinsky.

docs.title: Agentic Strategies and Patterns catalogue
docs.text:
A catalogue of recurring strategies, good patterns, and anti-patterns observed in a long-running multi-agent operations fleet. Each entry is derived from anonymised incident archetypes and operational experience.

The catalogue is organised by the concern it addresses: dispatch, memory, verification, build/deploy, and coordination rituals. It is intended to feed the paper set and the role taxonomy.

docs.title: Pattern 1.1: Role-based routing
docs.text:
Statement: Assign work to agents by responsibility, not by availability or message proximity.

How it works: A Dispatcher reads the task domain and routes to the agent whose role owns that domain: Builder for code, Runtime for infrastructure, Verifier for correctness gates, Researcher for options, Archivist for memory and guidance.

Why it helps: Clear ownership prevents multiple agents from making partial edits to the same artefact. It also makes misrouting auditable.

When it fails: When the Dispatcher routes based on keyword matching without confidence (see anti-pattern 1.2), or when boundaries between roles are vague.

Linked archetype: confident-misrouting

Example. A user asks the fleet to "fix the slow endpoint." The Dispatcher routes the task to the Runtime role for infrastructure diagnosis, rather than to the Builder, because the symptom points to a service health problem. The Builder is only pulled in once the Runtime role confirms the issue is in code.

docs.title: Pattern 1.2: Verifiable dispatch instruction
docs.text:
Statement: Every dispatch must include a concrete deliverable and a verification criterion.

How it works: Instead of asking an agent to “look at X,” the Dispatcher says: “Fix X so that test Y passes; do not commit until Z is green.”

Why it helps: Status updates can be faked or stalled; a verifiable deliverable forces closure.

Linked archetype: loop-without-progress

Example. The Dispatcher sends the Builder: "Update the health-check endpoint so that the fleet-wide liveness probe returns HTTP 200 within 2 seconds; do not mark the task complete until the Verifier reports the probe green on the staging fleet." The Builder cannot reply with a status update alone; the green/red verdict from the Verifier is the required deliverable.

docs.title: Anti-pattern 1.1: Status-only check-ins
docs.text:
Statement: A coordination ritual that asks agents for status but not for a deliverable.

Symptoms: Agents report next steps repeatedly without completing them; the same blocker appears in every report.

Consequence: The fleet keeps moving without making progress.

Linked archetype: loop-without-progress

Example. Every hour the Dispatcher asks the Builder for an update on a failing deploy. The Builder replies, "I will check the logs next," for six cycles. No log check happens, no artefact is produced, and the deploy remains broken because the ritual never required evidence of progress.

docs.title: Anti-pattern 1.2: Confident misrouting
docs.text:
Statement: A Dispatcher assigns work to a specialist based on assumptions that are not verified.

Symptoms: A build agent is asked to fix a runtime problem; a runtime agent is asked to edit code. The specialist proceeds anyway because the instruction does not allow declining.

Consequence: Subtle wrong outputs, wasted time, and blame diffusion.

Linked archetype: confident-misrouting

Example. The Dispatcher sees a runtime alert and assumes it is caused by a recent configuration change. It sends the Builder a request to revert the change. The Builder complies, but the real fault is a stale network rule managed by the Runtime role. The revert is harmless and irrelevant; the outage continues.

docs.title: Pattern 2.1: Externalise session state
docs.text:
Statement: Session affinity must survive replica restart and horizontal scaling.

How it works: Store session metadata in a shared service (e.g., Redis) with an in-memory fallback for single-process or degraded modes.

Why it helps: Agents can scale without losing conversation context; recovery becomes stateless.

Linked archetype: drifting-context

Example. A conversation spans ten turns. A deployment restarts the agent process mid-task. Because session metadata is held in a shared memory service, the restarted agent resumes the same conversation state. Without externalisation, the agent defaults to a generic identity and asks the user to restate the goal.

docs.title: Pattern 2.2: Explicit identity in shared tools
docs.text:
Statement: Tools that write shared memory must require the caller’s identity and the subject’s identity; defaults must not encode a single-agent assumption.

How it works: A memory tool rejects a write unless both the caller identity and the subject identity are supplied.

Why it helps: Prevents silent misrouting into the wrong memory bucket in multi-agent systems.

Linked archetype: silent-dependency-break

Example. The Archivist role maintains a memory tool. Early on, the tool defaulted to a generic bucket when the caller identity was omitted. One agent wrote critical runtime notes into that bucket, but another agent later queried a different bucket and found nothing. Requiring explicit identity fields on every write turned the hidden bug into a visible contract violation.

docs.title: Pattern 2.3: Local-first, shared-when-useful
docs.text:
Statement: Keep transient, per-agent state in local memory; promote only stable, cross-agent facts to shared memory.

How it works: Agents tag local memories by maturity: draft, private, or ready to share. Only ready memories are promoted to the shared store with source attribution.

Why it helps: Reduces noise in shared memory and protects sensitive or unverified context.

Linked archetype: silent-dependency-break

Example. A Researcher agent drafts three candidate approaches to a problem and labels them draft. Only the final recommended approach, once verified, is promoted to the shared store as a shareable fact with a source link. The earlier discarded drafts never pollute the memory that the Builder or Runtime roles query.

docs.title: Anti-pattern 2.1: Drifting context
docs.text:
Statement: Summarisation or process-local storage loses constraints, identity, or goal information that later agents need.

Symptoms: A restarted agent defaults to a generic identity; a long thread drifts from the original goal after repeated summarisation.

Consequence: Decisions are made with stale or incomplete context.

Linked archetype: drifting-context

Example. A long-running task is summarised to fit a context window. The summary drops a constraint: "do not restart the database." The next agent in the thread proposes a recovery step that requires a database restart. The original constraint is gone, and the proposal would have caused data loss.

docs.title: Anti-pattern 2.2: Silent dependency break
docs.text:
Statement: An agent changes shared state without notifying other agents that depend on it.

Symptoms: One agent writes to a different identity namespace, regenerates a shared secret, or changes a config file; other agents fail hours later with no obvious cause.

Consequence: Cascading failures, long debugging loops.

Linked archetype: silent-dependency-break

Example. A Runtime agent rotates a shared credential used by the Archivist to authenticate to the memory service. The rotation succeeds locally, but the Archivist is not informed. Hours later every memory lookup fails with an authentication error, and the failure is first noticed as unrelated "amnesia" in downstream tasks.

docs.title: Pattern 3.1: Gate before forward motion
docs.text:
Statement: No fleet-approved change moves to the next phase until a Verifier signs off on the current phase.

How it works: Each phase has written pass/fail criteria. The Verifier runs the criteria and posts a green/red verdict before the Dispatcher advances.

Why it helps: Prevents accumulating unverified debt across phases.

Linked archetype: tool-success-mission-failure

Example. A migration is split into three phases: render manifests, apply to staging, apply to production. The Verifier defines that the staging phase is green only when the health endpoint reports 200 for five minutes. The Dispatcher refuses to schedule production until the Verifier posts green; the Runtime role cannot bypass the gate.

docs.title: Pattern 3.2: Calibrate evaluation metrics to the task distribution
docs.text:
Statement: A success metric must distinguish between successful outcomes in the target workload.

How it works: If a scoring formula collapses to zero for common successful cases, rescale or re-parameterise it.

Why it helps: A metric that cannot distinguish success is worse than no metric.

Linked archetype: tool-success-mission-failure

Example. A benchmark scores agents on task completion time alone. Long but correct solutions receive the same score as failures. A tournament that compares two agents becomes meaningless because both fast and slow successes look identical to the metric. The scoring is recalibrated to reward correctness first and efficiency second.

docs.title: Anti-pattern 3.1: Tool success, mission failure
docs.text:
Statement: Each step succeeds, but the composed workflow fails because there is no end-to-end gate.

Symptoms: Template renders, build passes, tests pass, but the deployed system is broken; benchmark tasks score as failed despite producing correct outputs.

Consequence: False confidence; regressions discovered late.

Linked archetype: tool-success-mission-failure

Example. A deploy pipeline reports every step green: the template rendered, the container built, and the unit tests passed. But the rendered manifest dropped a required environment variable. The deployed service starts and then immediately crashes. No end-to-end health gate existed, so the failure surfaces in production instead of at the pipeline.

docs.title: Anti-pattern 3.2: Surface-level drift
docs.text:
Statement: Docs, scripts, and code examples diverge from the actual system shape.

Symptoms: New adopters follow a tutorial that uses flat config keys while the loader expects nested keys; tool names differ between README and runtime; quickstart emits URLs for a component that was removed.

Consequence: First-try failures, support load, erosion of trust.

Linked archetype: surface-level-drift

Example. A quickstart guide tells a new operator to run a setup command using one binary name, but the binary has been renamed. The tutorial also emits a URL for a reverse proxy that was removed two releases ago. Each document is internally consistent; together they are impossible to follow on the first try.

docs.title: Pattern 4.1: Coupled urgent-fix bundles ship together
docs.text:
Statement: When multiple fixes are tightly coupled, land them as one unit with a single merge/deploy/rollback plan.

How it works: The Builder writes a merge plan; the Runtime agent writes a deploy plan; the Verifier writes a rollback plan. The fleet reviews all three before execution.

Why it helps: Prevents partial landing where one fix is on master and the runtime is still down.

Linked archetype: loop-without-progress

Example. Three separate bugs each have a fix approved, but each sits on its own branch. The live system remains broken because no single plan coordinates merging, deploying, and verifying all three together. The fleet bundles them into one urgent-fix unit with a shared merge/deploy/rollback plan and lands them in one coordinated step.

docs.title: Pattern 4.2: Idempotent secret bootstrap
docs.text:
Statement: Live secrets must be created once and preserved across upgrades; rotation must be a separate, deliberate operation.

How it works: A bootstrap script creates missing secrets and skips existing ones. Routine upgrades do not pass secret values. A CI check verifies that every expected secret is present in the rendered manifest.

Why it helps: Stops deploy tools from accidentally invalidating credentials.

Linked archetype: ungoverned-secret-mutation

Example. A bootstrap script runs on a fresh environment and creates API keys, database passwords, and signing secrets only if they do not already exist. A routine upgrade later renders the manifest without passing secret values; the CI check confirms the secrets are still referenced. No rotation happens by accident during a normal deploy.

docs.title: Pattern 4.3: Render the desired state, never suppress it
docs.text:
Statement: In declarative deployment tools, always emit the resource manifest; use live lookups only to preserve existing values, not to gate emission.

How it works: Declarative templates always render the desired resource. A live lookup reads current values so the upgrade is a no-op; a retention policy prevents accidental deletion.

Why it helps: Declarative tools prune resources they no longer see. Suppressing a resource to “protect” it causes the exact failure it was meant to prevent.

Linked archetype: tool-success-mission-failure

Example. A deployment tool is configured to skip rendering a Secret when it already exists in the cluster, intending to protect live credentials. The tool interprets the suppressed manifest as "this Secret is no longer desired" and deletes it. The correct approach is to always render the Secret and use a live lookup to preserve its existing value.

docs.title: Anti-pattern 4.1: Ungoverned secret mutation
docs.text:
Statement: A helper script or automation regenerates credentials that downstream consumers already hold.

Symptoms: A render-values helper is run against an environment file; it regenerates API keys and passwords and applies them. Services start, but agents cannot authenticate.

Consequence: Auth outage, credential drift, manual recovery.

Linked archetype: ungoverned-secret-mutation

Example. A Runtime agent runs a values-rendering helper against an environment file to refresh a deployment. The helper regenerates API keys, database passwords, and signing secrets, and the agent applies the new values. Services restart with the fresh credentials, but other agents and stateful consumers still hold the old ones. Within minutes the fleet reports widespread authentication failures.

docs.title: Anti-pattern 4.2: Stale hand-off
docs.text:
Statement: One agent completes recovery or setup but leaves state bound to an environment that no longer exists.

Symptoms: A cluster is rebuilt, but storage volumes still point to the old node identity; a kubeconfig on a workstation is stale after cluster recreation.

Consequence: Stateful workloads cannot start; operators cannot see the cluster.

Linked archetype: stale-hand-off

Example. The Runtime role rebuilds a cluster after a node failure. The new cluster is healthy, but persistent volumes still reference the old node identity. When the Builder role schedules workloads, their storage cannot mount because the stateful layer is anchored to the previous world.

docs.title: Pattern 5.1: Single-owner skills and docs
docs.text:
Statement: Every piece of agent guidance has one canonical owner and location.

How it works: Skills carry frontmatter declaring the owner, canonical path, and related skills. Duplicates are retired or merged, not copied silently.

Why it helps: Prevents drift between copies; agents read the same guidance.

Linked archetype: surface-level-drift

Example. Two profiles each contain a skill that describes how to triage a failed deploy. One copy says to re-run the bootstrap step; the other says to skip it. Depending on which profile an agent loads, it takes opposite actions. The fleet adopts a rule that every skill carries frontmatter naming a single owner role, a canonical location, and the last review date. Duplicates are retired, and agents are instructed to reject guidance that lacks the owner tag.

docs.title: Pattern 5.2: Drive forward without permission stalls
docs.text:
Statement: The Dispatcher advances multi-phase work to the next gate, convening the fleet at milestones, and only escalates to the human on true blockers.

How it works: At the end of each phase, the Dispatcher asks: what is blocked, who owns it, and can the fleet decide? If yes, continue. If no, escalate.

Why it helps: Reduces human coordination load; keeps momentum.

Linked archetype: loop-without-progress

Example. A migration has five phases. At the end of phase two the Dispatcher convenes the Builder, Runtime, and Verifier roles. All three agree the staging gate is green and the rollback plan is current. The Dispatcher advances to phase three without waiting for a new human prompt. It only escalates when phase four surfaces an unresolved dependency.

docs.title: Pattern 5.3: Post-mission review as ritual
docs.text:
Statement: After every significant migration or incident, the fleet convenes a review and updates skills, runbooks, or conventions.

Why it helps: Captures lessons before they decay; turns incidents into durable capability.

Linked archetypes: all

Example. After a secret-rotation outage, the Runtime and Archivist roles convene a review. They update the skill that describes secret handling to require explicit opt-in and a re-seeding plan. They also add a CI check that fails if a render helper is run against a live environment file. The next similar task follows the updated guidance.

docs.title: Anti-pattern 5.1: Decisions without landing rituals
docs.text:
Statement: The fleet approves changes but does not define who merges, deploys, verifies, and rolls back.

Symptoms: Urgent fixes exist on branches weeks after approval while the main branch remains broken; everyone assumes someone else will land the work.

Consequence: Approved work rots; runtime and codebases diverge.

Linked archetype: loop-without-progress

Example. The fleet agrees that a critical fix is needed. The Builder writes the patch, but no one is assigned to merge it. The Runtime role assumes the Builder will merge; the Builder assumes the Runtime role will deploy. Two weeks later the patch is still on a branch and the production system is still broken.

docs.title: Bibliography
docs.text: Sanitised, publishable reference library for the research publication set. The internal workspace keeps working annotations and system-specific notes separately.

docs.title: Multi-agent systems and agentic workflows
docs.text:
• Wei, J., et al. (2022). Chain-of-thought prompting elicits reasoning in large language models. NeurIPS 2022. — Reasoning decomposition; relevant to how agents break down multi-step operational tasks.
  • Wang, L., et al. (2024). A survey on large language model based autonomous agents. Frontiers of Computer Science. — General survey of architectures and capabilities; provides context for role specialisation.
  • Xi, Z., et al. (2023). The rise and potential of large language model based agents: A survey. arXiv:2309.07864. — Surveys planning, memory, and tool use; motivates the memory and dispatch concerns in the field study.
  • Park, J. S., et al. (2023). Generative agents: Interactive simulacra of human behaviour. ACM UIST 2023. — Memory-stream architecture for long-running agents; compare to the externalised-session-state pattern.

docs.title: Autonomous operations and SRE
docs.text:
• STRATUS: Autonomous Cloud Reliability Engineering (IBM Research, arXiv:2506.02009, NeurIPS 2025). — Closest parallel to this work: an LLM-based multi-agent system for autonomous cloud reliability engineering. Cited to position the present paper as complementary (patterns and lessons) rather than competing.
  • Beyer, B., et al. (2016). Site Reliability Engineering: How Google Runs Production Systems. O'Reilly. — Source of gates, ownership, post-mortems, and error budgets; the SRE lineage in which the observed patterns sit.
  • Gawande, A. (2009). The Checklist Manifesto: How to Get Things Right. Metropolitan Books. — Checklist and ritual as coordination tools; relevant to verifiable dispatch and landing rituals.
  • Limoncelli, T. A., et al. (2014). The Practice of Cloud System Administration. Addison-Wesley. — Operational patterns for distributed systems; background for build/deploy/runtime sections.
  • Allspaw, J. (2015). The Field Guide to Understanding 'Human Error'. 3rd ed. Productivity Press. — Incident archetype framing; helps justify why interaction patterns matter more than root-cause labels.
  • Dekker, S. W. A. (2011). Drift into Failure: From Hunting Broken Components to Understanding Complex Systems. Ashgate. — Drift and erosion of safety constraints; directly relevant to the drifting-context anti-pattern.

docs.title: Memory for agents
docs.text:
• Park et al., 2023 (above) — Generative Agents memory stream: reflection, retrieval, importance scoring.
  • MemGPT / Letta, Zep — Persistent memory backends for conversational agents. Compare as product-layer memory systems, not as deployment infrastructure.
  • Open Knowledge Format (OKF) v0.1 — Google. File-format layer for portable structured knowledge; compare at format layer, not runtime layer.

docs.title: Evaluation
docs.text:
• Hendrycks, D., et al. (2021). Measuring massive multitask language understanding. ICLR 2021. — General LLM capability benchmark; contrast with fleet-level collaboration metrics.
  • Liu, X., et al. (2023). AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as agents. ICLR 2024. — Multi-task agent benchmark; relevant to Paper 5 (evaluation protocol).
  • Jimenez, C., et al. (2023). SWE-bench: Can language models resolve real-world GitHub issues? NeurIPS 2023 Datasets and Benchmarks. — End-to-end software-engineering evaluation; cited in §5.2 on metric calibration and end-to-end gates.
  • Zheng, L., et al. (2023). Judging LLM-as-a-judge with MT-bench and chatbot arena. NeurIPS 2023. — LLM-as-a-judge methods; relevant for evaluating agent outputs in Paper 5.

docs.title: Agent coordination frameworks and role models
docs.text:
• AutoGen / Multi-Agent Conversation Framework (Microsoft Research). — General conversation-based multi-agent framework; compare to the role-routing and dispatch patterns.
  • CrewAI — Role-based agent crews. — Directly comparable to the role taxonomy; note that CrewAI roles are often task-centric rather than operational.
  • DSPy — Declarative language model programming and optimisers. — Declarative program construction; compare to the explicit-contracts argument in the Discussion.

docs.title: Practitioner engineering
docs.text: • Nygard, M. T. (2018). Release It! Design and Deploy Production-Ready Software. 2nd ed. Pragmatic Bookshelf. — Stability patterns and deployment anti-patterns; background for §6.

docs.title: Incident Archetypes
docs.text: Anonymised, generalizable incident patterns observed in long-running agent collaboration. Each archetype captures the interaction pattern, root cause, and lesson without exposing implementation specifics.

docs.title: 1. Stale hand-off
docs.text:
Pattern: One agent completes a task and passes it to another, but the context or state it passes is incomplete, stale, or bound to an environment that no longer exists.

Root cause: Shared memory writes and reads are not synchronised around task boundaries; recovery procedures ignore stateful resource lifecycle.

Example archetype: A runtime agent rebuilds a cluster, but storage volumes remain bound to the old node identity. Stateful agents then fail because their shared state references a location that no longer exists.

Lesson: Define explicit hand-off checkpoints and require the receiving agent to acknowledge completeness before proceeding; include stateful resource lifecycle in recovery playbooks.

docs.title: 2. Confident misrouting
docs.text:
Pattern: A dispatcher or pipeline assigns work based on assumptions that are no longer true, and downstream agents proceed with high confidence.

Root cause: Dispatch logic lacks a verification step or confidence threshold for environmental preconditions.

Example archetype: A build/deploy pipeline assumes an image registry is running and trusted. It is neither, so pulls fail. The failure is attributed to the wrong component until the precondition is explicitly checked.

Lesson: Verify environmental preconditions before routing work; make the precondition check visible and auditable.

docs.title: 3. Tool success, mission failure
docs.text:
Pattern: Each individual tool call or step succeeds, but the overall workflow fails because calls were composed in the wrong order or with inconsistent assumptions.

Root cause: Orchestration treats per-step status as workflow success; no end-to-end validation gate exists.

Example archetype: A configuration template is edited, a commit is clean, and a build passes, but the rendered manifest is syntactically broken because one value was removed and never replaced. The broken manifest is only discovered when a downstream deploy step fails.

Lesson: Verify end-to-end outcomes, not just per-step status codes or clean diffs.

docs.title: 4. Drifting context
docs.text:
Pattern: A long-running workflow gradually diverges from its original goal as intermediate results are summarised, reinterpreted, or stored in a way that loses constraints or identity.

Root cause: Compaction or session storage loses information that later agents need; replicas do not share session state.

Example archetype: A session resolver stores conversation state in-process. A request routed to a different replica or a restarted process silently drops the active session, defaulting to a generic identity and forgetting the task context.

Lesson: Preserve immutable goal or identity artefacts and externalise session state before advertising scale or recovery.

docs.title: 5. Silent dependency break
docs.text:
Pattern: An agent changes a shared resource another agent depends on, causing failures elsewhere hours or days later. The change itself appeared successful.

Root cause: No ownership model for shared state; no change notification; defaults hide the impact.

Example archetype: A shared memory tool defaults to a generic identity bucket. Most agents write there, but one agent writes with a different identity. Other agents querying with the generic identity never see those memories. Later, a deploy script regenerates a shared secret, invalidating seeded credentials without warning.

Lesson: Assign write ownership and identity; broadcast changes through a shared event log or schema; reject ambiguous calls rather than silently defaulting.

docs.title: 6. Loop without progress
docs.text:
Pattern: Two or more agents repeatedly exchange messages without converging on a decision or output. Status is reported, but no verifiable work is completed.

Root cause: The coordination ritual asks for updates instead of concrete, verifiable next actions; no deadline or escalation trigger.

Example archetype: A dispatcher asks a specialist for a status update each hour. The specialist replies with the next planned step but never performs it. After several cycles, the dispatcher realises the original task is still stalled.

Lesson: Coordination check-ins must require a verifiable deliverable and a deadline; cap retries and escalate when no progress evidence appears.

docs.title: 7. Surface-level drift
docs.text:
Pattern: Documentation, scripts, and code examples diverge from the actual system shape. First-time adopters follow a path that looks correct but fails.

Root cause: Docs and scripts are not treated as testable artefacts; no single canonical source of truth.

Example archetype: Config examples use a flat key shape while the loader expects a nested shape; quickstart scripts emit URLs for a reverse proxy that was removed; tool names differ between docs and runtime. Each individual file is “correct” in isolation; together they are impossible to follow.

Lesson: Treat docs, scripts, and config examples as part of the product interface; keep one canonical example and verify it end-to-end.

docs.title: 8. Ungoverned secret mutation
docs.text:
Pattern: A helper script or automation regenerates credentials that downstream agents and services have already been seeded with. The mutation succeeds; auth breaks everywhere.

Root cause: Secret rendering and secret rotation are not separated; no guardrails prevent a render helper from overwriting live secrets.

Example archetype: A values-rendering script is run against an environment file. It regenerates API keys, database passwords, and signing secrets, then applies them. The API accepts the new keys, but agents and the database still hold the old ones, causing 401/403 across the fleet.

Lesson: Separate secret rendering from secret rotation; require explicit opt-in and a re-seeding plan before mutating live credentials.

docs.title: Relationship to the taxonomy
docs.text:
These archetypes map to roles and concerns in role taxonomy and provide the empirical basis for the papers. Notably:

  • Stale hand-off and drifting context are memory/session failures addressed by the Archivist role.
  • Confident misrouting and loop without progress are dispatcher failures.
  • Tool success, mission failure and surface-level drift are builder/verifier gaps.
  • Silent dependency break and ungoverned secret mutation are runtime/archivist cross-cutting concerns.

docs.title: Pattern Examples Supplement
docs.text: This supplement documents how the examples in the Agentic Strategies and Patterns catalogue were added and the style rules they follow. It is retained as background for reviewers and future contributors.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Add one short, stylised example to every pattern and anti-pattern in the catalogue. Keep the papers lean by cross-referencing the catalogue rather than embedding full scenarios inline.

docs.title: Why examples help
docs.text:
The catalogue describes strategies in abstract terms ("verifiable dispatch instruction", "status-only check-ins", "render the desired state"). For readers who have not lived inside a multi-agent operations fleet, these labels are easy to agree with and hard to apply. A single concrete illustration per pattern:

  • Anchors the abstract statement to a recognisable interaction.
  • Shows what a good dispatch message, verification gate, or memory write looks like in practice.
  • Makes anti-patterns feel costly by showing the failure mode, not just the consequence.
  • Reuses the anonymised incident archetypes already collected, so the added work is small.

docs.title: Style rules for examples
docs.text:
Each example in the catalogue follows these rules:

  • 40–80 words.
  • Uses only the stylised role names: Dispatcher, Builder, Runtime, Verifier, Researcher, Archivist.
  • Uses generic nouns: "a configuration store", "a shared memory service", "an environment file", "a rendered manifest".
  • Replaces specific failures with operational categories: "a helper script regenerated credentials" instead of naming the script or command.
  • Labelled clearly as an anonymised, stylised illustration.

docs.title: Pattern 1.2: Verifiable dispatch instruction
docs.text: Example. The Dispatcher sends the Builder: "Update the health-check endpoint so that the fleet-wide liveness probe returns HTTP 200 within 2 seconds; do not mark the task complete until the Verifier reports the probe green on the staging fleet." The Builder cannot reply with a status update alone; the green/red verdict from the Verifier is the required deliverable.

docs.title: Anti-pattern 4.1: Ungoverned secret mutation
docs.text: Example. A Runtime agent runs a values-rendering helper against an environment file to refresh a deployment. The helper regenerates API keys, database passwords, and signing secrets, and the agent applies the new values. Services restart with the fresh credentials, but other agents and stateful consumers still hold the old ones. Within minutes the fleet reports widespread authentication failures. The helper was designed for rendering, not rotation, and had no opt-in guard for live mutation.

docs.title: Pattern 5.1: Single-owner skills and docs
docs.text: Example. Two profiles each contain a skill that describes how to triage a failed deploy. One copy says to re-run the bootstrap step; the other says to skip it. Depending on which profile an agent loads, it takes opposite actions. The fleet adopts a rule that every skill carries frontmatter naming a single owner role, a canonical location, and the last review date. Duplicates are retired, and agents are instructed to reject guidance that lacks the owner tag.

docs.title: Risks and mitigations
docs.text: Risk Mitigation Examples leak internal detail Require charter review on every example; use the anonymised archetypes as the source. Catalogue becomes too long Cap each example at ~80 words; keep one example per pattern. Examples drift from the paper Treat the catalogue as the single source of truth; the paper links to it. Examples feel synthetic Explicitly label them as stylised illustrations; do not pretend they are verbatim logs.

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text: The catalogue now includes one example per pattern and anti-pattern. The papers retain only sentence-length illustrations and links to the catalogue.

docs.title: Role Taxonomy for Multi-Agent Operations Fleets
docs.text: A lightweight, role-oriented taxonomy derived from running long-lived agent fleets. Roles describe responsibilities, not necessarily one agent per role.

docs.title: Core roles
docs.text: Role Responsibility Key concerns Dispatcher Decides who does what, monitors progress, resolves ambiguity Routing, prioritisation, deadlock detection Builder Implements, modifies, and packages code or configurations Reproducibility, dependency hygiene, rollback Verifier Checks correctness, runs tests, audits outputs Coverage, false positives, integration gaps Researcher Gathers information, evaluates options, reduces uncertainty Source quality, stale data, over-research Runtime Operates infrastructure, keeps services alive Health checks, observability, incident response Archivist Maintains shared memory, context, continuity, and agent guidance. Retrieval accuracy, privacy, forgetting curves, skill/doc drift

docs.title: Cross-cutting concerns
docs.text:
• Memory: who reads, who writes, what is canonical
  • Ownership: which role is accountable when a hand-off fails
  • Observability: how the fleet knows its own state
  • Fallbacks: what happens when a role is unavailable or wrong

docs.title: Open questions
docs.text:
• Should the taxonomy distinguish human-in-the-loop roles separately?
  • How do roles map to existing frameworks (AutoGen, CrewAI, DSPy agents)?
  • Can role failure be predicted or instrumented?

docs.title: Agentic Strategies in a Six-Agent Operations Fleet: An Experience Report
docs.text: Abstract. I report on the operation of a long-running fleet organised around six stable operational roles: Dispatcher, Builder, Runtime, Verifier, Researcher, and Archivist. In my implementation, each role was usually assigned to a distinct agent instance, but the patterns here concern responsibilities rather than process topology. Over an extended period I collected operational failures, near-misses, and coordination breakdowns, de-identified them into incident archetypes, and distilled the recurring coordination strategies and anti-patterns that shaped fleet reliability. I organise these into five concerns: dispatch, memory and shared state, verification, build/deploy/runtime, and coordination rituals. For each concern I present concrete patterns drawn from anonymised incident archetypes and discuss why they matter for long-running multi-agent operations systems. The paper is intended as an experience report for practitioners and as groundwork for a more formal taxonomy of agent collaboration; I do not claim statistical prevalence across fleets, only that these patterns recurred in mine and are plausible elsewhere.

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Multi-agent systems are increasingly used for long-running operational work: maintaining infrastructure, writing and verifying code, researching options, and remembering context across sessions. Much of the research literature focuses on single-turn tool use, benchmark performance, or small cooperative tasks. Less is written about what happens when agents run for weeks or months, coordinate through shared state, and own distinct operational responsibilities.

I operated a fleet organised around six stable roles. In my implementation, each role was usually assigned to a distinct agent instance, but the patterns here concern the responsibilities, not the process topology:

  • Dispatcher — decides who does what, monitors progress, resolves ambiguity.
  • Builder — implements, modifies, and packages code or configuration.
  • Runtime — operates infrastructure and keeps services alive.
  • Verifier — checks correctness, runs tests, and audits outputs.
  • Researcher — gathers information, evaluates options, and reduces uncertainty.
  • Archivist — maintains shared memory, context, continuity, and agent guidance.

This paper extracts the strategies and anti-patterns I observed. I deliberately avoid system-specific details; all examples are anonymised and generalised. Many of the patterns echo long-standing practices in site reliability engineering and incident management: checklists, ownership, gates, post-mortems, and immutable desired state. My contribution is not to introduce those practices but to show how they manifest, fail, and must be re-instrumented when the operators are LLM agents coordinating through shared state and tools.

My contribution is observational: a catalogue of recurring coordination behaviours that other practitioners can adopt, test, or contradict in their own fleets. I do not claim statistical prevalence across all agent fleets. I claim only that these patterns recurred in mine and that each is plausible in other long-running agent systems.

docs.title: 2. Method: from incidents to patterns
docs.text: My analysis followed three steps. I treat the result as an observational experience report, not a controlled study.

docs.title: 2.1 Setting
docs.text: The fleet handled routine infrastructure, code, documentation, and research tasks over an extended period. In my implementation the six roles listed in §1 were usually assigned to distinct agent instances supervised by a human operator. The operator acted as a final arbiter for high-risk actions such as credential rotation, production deploys, and architectural changes; routine work within defined guardrails was left to the fleet. Concrete, anonymised illustrations of each pattern are collected in the agentic patterns catalogue.

docs.title: 2.2 Data collection
docs.text:
I recorded operational failures, near-misses, and coordination breakdowns as they happened. These ranged from configuration drift and authentication outages to loops where work was repeatedly discussed but not completed. Tens of incidents were logged and distilled into eight recurring archetypes (see incident archetypes). I de-identified each incident before analysis: I removed product names, hostnames, dates, and internal identifiers, retaining only the interaction pattern, root-cause category, and lesson.

For example, an internal incident in which a values-rendering script regenerated live credentials became the archetype ungoverned secret mutation: a helper script or automation regenerates credentials that downstream agents and services have already been seeded with, causing a fleet-wide authentication outage.

docs.title: 2.3 Analysis
docs.text:
For each archetype I asked two questions:

  1. Did a positive counter-strategy exist in the fleet?
  2. Could that counter-strategy generalise beyond my specific system?

If both answers were yes, I recorded the result as a pattern. I also recorded anti-patterns where no reliable counter-strategy had yet stabilised. The final catalogue contains twenty-two patterns and anti-patterns grouped by concern, each with a short anonymised example (see agentic patterns catalogue).

docs.title: 2.4 Threats to validity
docs.text: This method has well-known limitations. My incident logs were biased toward memorable failures rather than routine successes. I also built and operated the system, so observer effects and hindsight bias are likely. The fleet is a single system, and its task mix, tooling, and human oversight model may not match other deployments. I therefore make no claim of statistical prevalence across agent fleets; I claim only that these patterns recurred in mine and that each is plausible elsewhere.

docs.title: 3. Dispatch: routing work to the right agent
3.1 Strategy: role-based routing
docs.text:
In the fleet, the dispatch strategy that recurred most reliably was simple: route by responsibility. The Dispatcher did not ask “who is free?” but “who owns this domain?” A build task went to the Builder; an infrastructure task to the Runtime; a correctness task to the Verifier; a research task to the Researcher; a memory, continuity, or guidance task to the Archivist.

This strategy correlated with fewer uncoordinated edits to the same artefact. It also made ownership legible: when something failed, the first question was whether the right role had been dispatched, not which agent had touched the code last.

docs.title: 3.2 Strategy: verifiable dispatch instruction
docs.text:
A second dispatch strategy that proved durable for us was to make every dispatch contain a concrete deliverable and a verification criterion. Instead of “look into X,” the routine evolved to: “Fix X so that test Y passes; do not report completion until Z is green.”

This was a direct response to status-only check-ins, in which agents would report next steps repeatedly without closing the task. Verifiable instructions shifted the conversation from activity to evidence.

docs.title: 3.3 Anti-pattern: status-only check-ins
docs.text:
Among the dispatch anti-patterns I observed, the costliest was the status-only check-in. A coordination ritual asked agents for status at fixed intervals. Each agent reported what it planned to do next. No one verified that the previous next step had actually happened. After several cycles the same blocker appeared unchanged, merely re-described.

The lesson: coordination rituals must require evidence of progress, not just reports of intent.

docs.title: 3.4 Anti-pattern: confident misrouting
docs.text:
A second dispatch anti-pattern was routing based on unverified assumptions. For example, a build role was asked to fix a runtime problem because the Dispatcher assumed the issue was in a recently changed file. The build role proceeded because the instruction did not allow it to decline or escalate. The result was a technically correct edit that did not address the actual failure.

The lesson: dispatch decisions should be auditable and reversible; specialists should be able to decline or escalate when the assignment is outside their domain.

docs.title: 4. Memory and shared state: preserving context and identity
4.1 Strategy: externalise session state
docs.text:
Session state is easy to store in-process. That works until an agent restarts, scales horizontally, or a request lands on a different replica. I learned that session affinity must survive process boundaries.

The strategy was to store session metadata in a shared service with an in-memory fallback for single-process or degraded operation. This allowed the system to scale without losing conversation context and made recovery stateless.

docs.title: 4.2 Strategy: explicit identity in shared tools
docs.text:
Shared memory tools defaulted to a generic identity early in the project. That was convenient for a single agent but silently wrong for a fleet. Agents wrote memories into a bucket that other agents did not query.

The fix was to require explicit caller and subject identity on every write. The tool rejected calls missing identity fields rather than silently defaulting. This turned a hidden correctness bug into an explicit contract.

docs.title: 4.3 Strategy: local-first, shared-when-useful
docs.text:
Not every memory belongs in the shared store. I adopted a convention: local memory holds transient scaffolding, drafts, and per-agent preferences; shared memory holds stable, cross-agent facts. Agents tag local memories as shareable, draft, private, or local-only, and only promote shareable facts with source attribution.

This reduced noise in shared memory and prevented sensitive or unverified context from leaking across agents.

docs.title: 4.4 Anti-pattern: drifting context
docs.text:
A recurring anti-pattern was losing context through summarisation or process-local storage. A long-running workflow would be summarised for compactness, and the summary would drop a constraint. Or a session resolver would store state in-process, so a restarted agent would default to a generic identity and forget the active task.

The lesson: preserve immutable goal or identity artefacts and externalise session state before advertising scale or recovery.

docs.title: 4.5 Anti-pattern: silent dependency break
docs.text:
Agents often changed shared state without considering downstream consumers. One agent would write to a different identity namespace; another would regenerate a shared credential; a third would change a config file. Downstream agents failed hours later with no obvious cause.

The lesson: assign write ownership and identity; broadcast changes through a shared event log or schema; reject ambiguous calls rather than silently defaulting.

docs.title: 5. Verification: proving the system is right
5.1 Strategy: gate before forward motion
docs.text:
The verification strategy that proved most effective for us was to block forward motion until a Verifier signed off on the current phase. Each phase had written pass/fail criteria. The Verifier ran them and posted a green/red verdict. The Dispatcher did not advance until the gate was green.

This prevented unverified debt from accumulating across phases. It also forced the Verifier to publish criteria before work started, reducing subjective “looks fine” sign-offs.

docs.title: 5.2 Strategy: calibrate evaluation metrics to the task distribution
docs.text:
A benchmark suite initially used a fitness formula that collapsed to zero for long but successful tasks. The metric could not distinguish between a fast success and a slow success, making tournament-mode comparison impossible.

The lesson: a metric that cannot distinguish successful outcomes is worse than no metric. I recalibrated the scoring function to the actual task distribution.

docs.title: 5.3 Anti-pattern: tool success, mission failure
docs.text:
A pervasive anti-pattern was treating per-step success as workflow success. Every individual call succeeded — the template rendered, the build passed, the tests passed — but the deployed system was broken because no end-to-end gate existed. In one case a configuration template was edited but a value was removed and never replaced; the build was clean, but the rendered manifest was syntactically broken.

The lesson: verify end-to-end outcomes, not just per-step status codes or clean diffs.

docs.title: 5.4 Anti-pattern: surface-level drift
docs.text:
Documentation, scripts, and code examples drifted from the actual system shape. New adopters followed a tutorial that used a flat config shape while the loader expected a nested shape. Tool names differed between the README and the runtime. A quickstart emitted URLs for a reverse proxy that had been removed.

Each file was internally consistent. Together they were impossible to follow. The lesson: treat docs, scripts, and config examples as part of the product interface; keep one canonical example and verify it end-to-end.

docs.title: 6. Build, deploy, and runtime: landing changes safely
6.1 Strategy: coupled bundles ship together
docs.text:
When several urgent fixes are tightly coupled, splitting them across releases creates a different risk than bundling them. I learned to ship coupled urgent-fix bundles as one unit with three artefacts: a merge plan (Builder), a deploy plan (Runtime), and a rollback plan (Verifier). The fleet reviewed all three before execution.

This addressed a recurring failure in which fixes were approved but sat on branches while the live system remained broken. Everyone assumed someone else would land the work.

docs.title: 6.2 Strategy: idempotent secret bootstrap
docs.text:
Credentials are a common source of accidental outage. I separated secret creation from routine upgrades. A bootstrap script created missing secrets and skipped existing ones. Routine upgrades did not pass secret values. A CI check verified that every expected secret was present in the rendered manifest.

The principle: secret rotation and secret rendering are different operations and must be guarded differently.

docs.title: 6.3 Strategy: render the desired state, never suppress it
docs.text:
In a declarative deployment system, I once tried to “protect” live secrets by suppressing their manifests when they already existed. The tool interpreted the suppressed manifest as “these resources are no longer desired” and pruned them. The outage was caused by a guard meant to prevent exactly that.

The correct pattern is to always render the desired state and use live lookups only to preserve existing values. The declarative tool should see the resource every time.

docs.title: 6.4 Anti-pattern: ungoverned secret mutation
docs.text:
A helper script intended for rendering values was run against a live environment file. It regenerated API keys, passwords, and signing secrets, and applied them. Services restarted with the new credentials, but agents and the database still held the old ones. The result was a fleet-wide authentication outage.

The lesson: separate secret rendering from secret rotation; require explicit opt-in and a re-seeding plan before mutating live credentials.

docs.title: 6.5 Anti-pattern: stale hand-off
docs.text:
One agent rebuilt a cluster but left storage volumes bound to the old node identity. Another agent then tried to deploy workloads, which failed because their PVCs could not mount. The cluster looked healthy, but the stateful layer was anchored to a previous world.

The lesson: recovery procedures must include the lifecycle of stateful resources, not just pods and services.

docs.title: 7. Coordination rituals: keeping the fleet coherent
7.1 Strategy: single-owner skills and docs
docs.text: Agent instructions are executable code. When the same guidance existed in multiple overlapping skills across different profiles, outputs varied because agents were reading conflicting copies. I adopted a single-owner convention: every skill has one canonical profile, path, and owner. Duplicates are retired or merged, not copied silently.

docs.title: 7.2 Strategy: drive forward without permission stalls
docs.text:
The operational routine evolved so that the Dispatcher advanced multi-phase work to the next gate without waiting for a fresh human prompt at every step. At each milestone it asked: what is blocked, who owns it, and can the fleet decide? If yes, it continued. If no, it escalated to the human.

This reduced coordination load and kept momentum, but it required the other strategies — especially verifiable dispatch and gate-before-forward-motion — to avoid turning autonomy into drift.

docs.title: 7.3 Strategy: post-mission review as ritual
docs.text: After every significant migration or incident, the fleet convened a review and updated skills, runbooks, or conventions. This turned incidents into durable capability rather than one-off fixes.

docs.title: 7.4 Anti-pattern: decisions without landing rituals
docs.text:
A recurring anti-pattern was approving changes without defining who would merge, deploy, verify, and roll back. The decision existed on paper but never reached the running system. Approved work rotted while the fleet argued about sequence.

The lesson: a decision is not done until it has a merge plan, a deploy plan, a verification gate, and a rollback plan.

docs.title: 8. Discussion
docs.text:
The patterns above share a common theme: agent collaboration fails when implicit assumptions cross boundaries. A default identity, a stale configuration context, a suppressed manifest, a status-only check-in — each is a small assumption that becomes a large failure when it is not shared, verified, or owned.

The strategies that worked for us are mostly about making boundaries explicit:

  • Roles make ownership explicit.
  • Verifiable instructions make progress explicit.
  • Explicit identity in shared tools makes authorship explicit.
  • End-to-end gates make correctness explicit.
  • Merge/deploy/rollback plans make landing explicit.

My experience suggests that clearer contracts between agents may be at least as important as increasing the capability of individual agents. I treat this as a working hypothesis, not a proven causal claim; testing it in other fleets is an open question.

The patterns are also hypotheses. Their value is in making implicit coordination assumptions explicit enough to be tested, instrumented, or contradicted in other long-running agent systems.

docs.title: 9. Limitations and future work
docs.text:
This is a single-system experience report. The patterns may not generalise to all agent fleets. I did not run controlled experiments; I distilled observations from incident logs. My incident logs are biased toward memorable failures, and my involvement in building and operating the fleet introduces observer effects and hindsight bias. Future work could:

  • Quantify how often each anti-pattern occurs in agent logs.
  • Build a lightweight benchmark that exercises the patterns in agentic patterns catalogue.
  • Develop a formal role taxonomy that maps these patterns to existing frameworks (AutoGen, CrewAI, DSPy).

I am pursuing the latter two in companion papers in this set.

docs.title: 10. Conclusion
docs.text:
I presented an experience report on agentic strategies in a six-role operations fleet. The strategies that I found effective — role-based routing, verifiable dispatch, externalised session state, explicit identity, gate-before-forward-motion, coupled-bundle landing, idempotent secret bootstrap, single-owner skills, and forward-drive coordination — all make implicit assumptions explicit. The anti-patterns that repeatedly failed — status-only check-ins, confident misrouting, drifting context, silent dependency breaks, tool-success mission failures, surface-level drift, ungoverned secret mutation, stale hand-offs, and decisions without landing rituals — all allowed assumptions to cross boundaries unchecked.

I offer this catalogue to practitioners and researchers as a starting point for building more reliable long-running agent systems. I encourage readers to treat the patterns as testable hypotheses rather than proven prescriptions.

docs.title: Acknowledgements
docs.text: I thank the operators and the systems whose repeated failures made these patterns visible.

docs.title: References
docs.text: See bibliography.

docs.title: Failure Modes in Long-Running Agent Collaboration
docs.text: Abstract. I report on the failure modes observed while operating a multi-agent operations fleet over an extended period. The fleet was organised around six stable roles: Dispatcher, Builder, Runtime, Verifier, Researcher, and Archivist. Failures did not usually come from a single bad tool call; they emerged when coordination assumptions crossed role boundaries and were not shared, verified, or owned. I group the recurring failures into five classes: memory and identity, dispatch and routing, build/deploy/runtime, documentation and surface-level drift, and evaluation and metrics. For each class I describe the symptoms, root cause, and the ritual or contract that eventually contained it. The paper is intended as an experience report for practitioners building long-running agent systems and as a complement to the companion field study of agentic strategies.

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Much attention in multi-agent systems research is paid to cooperation: how agents share tasks, negotiate, or jointly solve problems. Less is written about what fails when agents operate continuously, share state, and inherit responsibilities from previous sessions. In a long-running fleet, a single tool call can be correct while the surrounding coordination is wrong. The failure is often not in the model but in the contract between agents.

I operated a fleet organised around six stable roles. Over an extended period I recorded operational failures, near-misses, and coordination breakdowns, de-identified them into incident archetypes, and grouped them by the boundary that broke. This paper focuses on what went wrong and what containment strategies proved useful. The companion field study paper focuses on the corresponding positive patterns.

docs.title: 2. Method
docs.text:
The incidents in this paper come from the same operational logs as the companion field study. I followed three steps:

  1. Collect incidents. I recorded failures and near-misses as they happened, noting the roles involved and the symptom that was first visible.
  2. Extract archetypes. I removed system-specific identifiers and retained only the interaction pattern, root-cause category, and lesson. The full set of eight archetypes is in the incident archetypes catalogue.
  3. Group by boundary. I asked which boundary the archetype crossed: memory, dispatch, runtime, documentation, or evaluation. This produced the five failure classes below.

This is a single-system, qualitative experience report. The fleet operated within human-supervised guardrails, as described in the companion field study; the failures below occurred when implicit contracts failed despite that oversight. I make no claim of statistical prevalence across all agent fleets; I claim only that these failure classes recurred in mine and are plausible in other long-running systems.

docs.title: 3.1 Memory and identity failures
docs.text:
The most persistent failures involved shared memory. An agent would read or write state under the wrong identity, a session would be lost across restarts, or a summarised conversation would drop a constraint.

Stale hand-off. One agent completed a recovery step but left state bound to an environment that no longer existed. The new environment looked healthy, but stateful resources still pointed to the old environment identity. The next agent tried to schedule workloads, and storage could not attach. The compute layer was fine; the stateful layer was not.

Example. A Runtime role rebuilt a compute environment after a failure. The new environment passed health checks, but the storage layer still referenced the old environment identity. When the Builder role scheduled a stateful workload, the workload could not attach its data. The failure appeared as a storage error, not as a recovery misstep.

Drifting context. A long conversation or workflow was summarised for compactness, and the summary dropped a goal or constraint. A later agent then proposed an action that violated the original requirement. The constraint had not been explicitly contradicted; it had silently disappeared.

Example. A Researcher role spent twelve turns comparing deployment strategies for a service that must remain available. The thread was summarised to fit the context window, and the summary omitted the availability constraint. The next agent proposed a strategy that required a brief outage. The original requirement was not disputed; it had been compressed away.

Silent dependency break. An agent changed a shared value without notifying downstream consumers. A credential was rotated, an identity namespace was changed, or a config file was rewritten. Hours later, other agents failed with symptoms that did not obviously trace back to the change.

Example. A Runtime role rotated a credential used by the Archivist role to write to the shared memory service. The rotation was reported as successful locally, but no event was broadcast. Hours later, every memory lookup began failing with an authentication error, and the symptom was first interpreted as unrelated "amnesia" in downstream tasks.

The common root cause was that memory boundaries were implicit. There was no canonical identity for reads and writes, no immutable artefact for the original goal, and no event log for changes.

docs.title: 3.2 Dispatch and routing failures
docs.text:
Failures in this class were not usually caused by a bad model answer; they were caused by the wrong specialist receiving the wrong task under the wrong assumptions.

Confident misrouting. A Dispatcher routed work based on an unverified assumption. A build role was asked to fix a runtime problem because the failure appeared near a recently changed file. The build role produced a technically correct edit that did not address the actual fault.

Example. A health check began failing shortly after a configuration change. The Dispatcher assumed the change was at fault and sent the Builder role a request to revert it. The Builder reverted the change, but the real issue was a stale route in the network layer, owned by the Runtime role. The revert was harmless and irrelevant; the outage continued.

Loop without progress. A coordination ritual asked agents for status at fixed intervals. Each agent reported what it planned to do next, but no one verified that the previous step had actually happened. After several cycles the same blocker appeared, merely re-described.

Example. Every hour the Dispatcher asked the Builder for an update on a failing deployment. The Builder replied, "I will inspect the logs next," for six consecutive cycles. No log inspection was produced, no artefact changed, and the deployment remained broken because the ritual never required evidence of progress.

Both cases shared a root cause: the dispatch contract did not require evidence. The instruction either lacked a concrete deliverable or lacked a mechanism to decline or escalate when the assignment was outside the specialist’s domain.

docs.title: 3.3 Build, deploy, and runtime failures
docs.text:
These failures happened when changes were partially landed, secrets were mutated without a re-seeding plan, or declarative tooling was used against its own semantics.

Ungoverned secret mutation. A helper script intended for rendering values was run against a live environment file. It regenerated API keys, database passwords, and signing secrets, and applied them. Services restarted with new credentials, but other agents and stateful consumers still held the old ones. The result was a fleet-wide authentication outage.

Example. A Runtime role ran a values-rendering helper against an environment file to refresh a deployment preview. The helper regenerated every credential in the file and applied the new values. Services using the new credentials started, but the Archivist and several downstream consumers still held the old credentials. Within minutes, authentication failures spread across the fleet.

Tool success, mission failure. Every step in a deploy pipeline reported green: the template rendered, the build passed, the tests passed. But the rendered manifest was missing a required value, and the deployed service crashed immediately. No end-to-end health gate existed, so the failure surfaced in production rather than in the pipeline.

Example. A deployment pipeline reported green for rendering, building, and unit tests. The Runtime role promoted the result to the live environment. The service started and immediately crashed because a required environment variable was absent from the rendered manifest. No end-to-end health check existed between the test suite and the live deploy.

Stale hand-off also appears in this class when one agent’s setup output is consumed by another agent hours later in an environment that has since changed.

The common root cause was that landing rituals were incomplete. A decision existed on paper but had no merge plan, deploy plan, verification gate, or rollback plan.

docs.title: 3.4 Documentation and surface-level drift
docs.text:
Documentation, scripts, and code examples drifted from the actual system shape. Each file was internally consistent, but together they were impossible to follow.

Surface-level drift. A quickstart guide used flat configuration keys while the loader expected nested keys. A tutorial emitted URLs for a reverse proxy that had been removed. Tool names differed between the README and the runtime. New adopters followed a path that looked correct but failed.

Example. A quickstart told a new operator to run a setup command using one binary name, but the command-line tool had been renamed. The same guide emitted a URL for a reverse proxy component that had been removed two releases earlier. Each document was internally consistent; together they were impossible to follow on the first attempt.

The root cause was that guidance was not treated as a testable interface. No single owner role was accountable for keeping examples current, and no end-to-end verification gate checked that the quickstart actually worked.

docs.title: 3.5 Evaluation and metric failures
docs.text:
Not all failures were runtime outages. Some were failures of measurement.

A benchmark suite used a fitness formula that collapsed to zero for long but successful tasks. The metric could not distinguish a fast success from a slow success, making comparison meaningless. In my experience, a metric that cannot distinguish successful outcomes is worse than no metric, because it creates false confidence while hiding real differences.

Example. A benchmark compared two agents on task completion. The scoring function rewarded speed but collapsed to zero whenever a task exceeded a threshold, regardless of whether the final answer was correct. A slow but correct agent received the same score as an agent that failed outright. The metric could not distinguish competence from failure.

The root cause was that the metric was not calibrated to the actual task distribution. The evaluation contract assumed that execution time alone captured task quality.

docs.title: 4. Composite failure chains
docs.text:
The classes above rarely occurred in isolation. A typical chain looked like this:

  1. A Dispatcher confidently misrouted a task to a build role because an environmental precondition was not verified.
  2. The build role made a change that touched shared configuration, but the change was not broadcast because identity and ownership were implicit.
  3. A downstream agent failed hours later. The initial routing error and the silent dependency break combined into an outage.
  4. During recovery, documentation was found to be out of date, so the operator followed a stale quickstart and prolonged the incident.

The lesson is that small boundary failures compound. In my experience, a single missing contract can turn a local mistake into a fleet-wide, long-duration incident.

docs.title: 5. Containment strategies
docs.text:
For each failure class, a containment strategy eventually emerged:

  • Memory and identity: externalise session state, require explicit identity on every shared-memory write, and keep an immutable goal artefact.
  • Dispatch and routing: route by responsibility, include a concrete deliverable and verification criterion, and allow specialists to decline or escalate.
  • Build/deploy/runtime: land coupled fixes as one bundle, separate secret rendering from rotation, and always render the desired state in declarative tools.
  • Documentation: treat quickstarts and examples as testable interfaces with a single owner role.
  • Evaluation: calibrate metrics to the actual task distribution and verify end-to-end outcomes.

These are described in detail, with examples, in the agentic patterns catalogue.

docs.title: 6. Limitations and future work
docs.text: This is a single-system experience report. The failure classes may not cover all multi-agent failure modes. The logs are biased toward memorable incidents, and my involvement in building the fleet introduces observer bias. Future work could quantify how often each failure class occurs in agent logs and build lightweight benchmarks that exercise them. I am pursuing the latter in the companion evaluation-protocol paper.

docs.title: 7. Conclusion
docs.text: I presented five classes of failure observed in a long-running multi-agent operations fleet: memory and identity, dispatch and routing, build/deploy/runtime, documentation drift, and evaluation metrics. In each case, the failure was less about an individual tool call and more about a missing or implicit contract between agents. The containment strategies all make boundaries explicit: identity, ownership, deliverables, landing rituals, and measurement criteria. I offer this taxonomy to practitioners and researchers as a starting point for building more reliable long-running agent systems.
