docs.text: See bibliography.

docs.title: A Role-Oriented Taxonomy for Multi-Agent Systems
docs.text: Abstract. I propose a lightweight, role-oriented taxonomy for long-running multi-agent operations systems. The taxonomy defines six roles by responsibility rather than by agent instance: Dispatcher, Builder, Runtime, Verifier, Researcher, and Archivist. I identify four cross-cutting concerns that constrain every role—memory, ownership, observability, and fallbacks—and map the patterns from a companion field study onto the roles they most affect. The taxonomy is intended as a design tool for practitioners and as a step toward more comparative research in multi-agent coordination.

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
Multi-agent systems are often described by their number of agents, their model provider, or their conversation protocol. These descriptions tell us little about who is responsible for what. In a long-running operations fleet, what matters is not how many agents there are but whether the right responsibility is applied to the right problem at the right time.

I operated a fleet organised around six stable roles. In my implementation each role was usually assigned to a distinct agent instance, but the taxonomy itself is about responsibilities, not process topology. This paper defines the six roles, identifies the concerns that cut across all of them, and maps observed coordination patterns to the roles they most affect.

docs.title: 2. Related work
docs.text:
Existing multi-agent frameworks such as AutoGen, CrewAI, and DSPy provide conversation structure, role-based crews, and declarative programming tools. They do not, however, prescribe how roles behave over months of operation, how they share persistent memory, or how they recover when a role is unavailable or wrong.

Site reliability engineering and incident-management literature provides mature concepts of ownership, gates, post-mortems, and error budgets. My contribution is not to introduce those concepts but to adapt them to a setting where the operators are LLM agents with bounded context and tool access.

docs.title: Dispatcher
docs.text:
Responsibility: decides who does what, monitors progress, resolves ambiguity.

The Dispatcher is the only role whose primary job is coordination. It does not implement, verify, or operate directly; it routes tasks, detects stalls, and escalates when the fleet cannot resolve a blocker. A good Dispatcher asks, “Who owns this domain?” not “Who is free?”

Example. A user reports that "the service is slow." The Dispatcher routes the task to the Runtime role for health and infrastructure diagnosis, not to the Builder role for code changes. Only after the Runtime role confirms the bottleneck is in code does the Dispatcher pull in the Builder.

Key failure mode: confident misrouting, loops without progress.

docs.title: Builder
docs.text:
Responsibility: implements, modifies, and packages code or configuration.

The Builder turns decisions into artefacts. It writes patches, updates manifests, and produces the deliverables that other roles consume. Its work is most visible, which makes it a frequent target of misrouted tasks.

Example. The Builder receives a verifiable dispatch: "Update the health-check endpoint so the fleet-wide liveness probe returns HTTP 200 within 2 seconds. Do not mark complete until the Verifier reports the probe green on the staging fleet." The Builder’s deliverable is the green/red verdict from the Verifier, not a status update.

Key failure mode: being asked to fix runtime or verification problems outside its domain.

docs.title: Runtime
docs.text:
Responsibility: operates infrastructure and keeps services alive.

The Runtime role owns deploys, restarts, health checks, and incident response. It is closest to the live system and therefore the natural owner of secrets, stateful resources, and recovery playbooks.

Example. After a compute environment is rebuilt, the Runtime role must ensure stateful resources point to the new environment identity before the Builder schedules workloads. If the identity hand-off is skipped, healthy-looking compute will fail to attach storage.

Key failure mode: stale hand-offs after recovery, ungoverned secret mutation.

docs.title: Verifier
docs.text:
Responsibility: checks correctness, runs tests, and audits outputs.

The Verifier defines green/red criteria and posts verdicts. It is not a generic critic; it is a gatekeeper with written, phase-specific criteria that the fleet uses to advance work.

Example. A migration has three phases: render manifests, apply to staging, apply to production. The Verifier declares the staging phase green only when the health endpoint reports HTTP 200 for five minutes. The Dispatcher will not schedule production until that verdict is posted.

Key failure mode: treating per-step tool success as mission success without an end-to-end gate.

docs.title: Researcher
docs.text:
Responsibility: gathers information, evaluates options, and reduces uncertainty.

The Researcher role reduces unknowns before the Dispatcher commits the fleet to a path. It evaluates alternatives, finds precedents, and flags risks. It does not own implementation or verification.

Example. Before choosing a secret-rotation strategy, the Researcher compares three approaches, notes that one requires downtime, and produces a decision artefact: "Option C is safest because it keeps rotation and re-seeding explicit." The Dispatcher then routes implementation to the Runtime and Builder roles.

Key failure mode: over-researching without producing a decision artefact, or being routed implementation work.

docs.title: Archivist
docs.text:
Responsibility: maintains shared memory, context, continuity, and agent guidance.

The Archivist role decides what is remembered, how it is retrieved, and who can read or write it. It owns the shared-memory contract: identity, namespaces, promotion rules, and skill libraries.

Example. The Archivist role maintains the skill library. When two profiles contain conflicting copies of the same triage skill, the Archivist retires the duplicate, keeps one canonical copy with owner metadata, and updates the guidance so agents reject untagged skills.

Key failure mode: drifting context, silent dependency breaks, surface-level drift in the guidance library.

docs.title: 4. Cross-cutting concerns
docs.text:
Four concerns affect every role:

  • Memory. Who reads, who writes, what is canonical, and how identity is preserved across sessions. Without explicit answers, agents write into incompatible buckets or lose context.
  • Ownership. Which role is accountable when a hand-off fails. Clear ownership prevents blame diffusion and makes misrouting auditable.
  • Observability. How the fleet knows its own state. Every role must be able to report a status that another role can verify.
  • Fallbacks. What happens when a role is unavailable, wrong, or overloaded. Fallbacks prevent confident misrouting from becoming a hard failure.

Example. A Dispatcher is unavailable. Because ownership and fallbacks are explicit, the fleet knows the Builder can still execute pre-approved verifiable tasks and escalate only on new blockers, rather than stall or guess.

docs.title: 5. Role-to-pattern mapping
docs.text:
The patterns from the companion field study map to roles as follows:

Pattern / Anti-pattern Primary role Cross-cutting concern Role-based routing Dispatcher Ownership Verifiable dispatch instruction Dispatcher Observability Status-only check-ins Dispatcher Observability Confident misrouting Dispatcher Ownership, Fallbacks Externalise session state Archivist Memory Explicit identity in shared tools Archivist Memory, Ownership Local-first, shared-when-useful Archivist Memory Drifting context Archivist Memory Silent dependency break Archivist Memory, Ownership Gate before forward motion Verifier Observability Calibrate evaluation metrics Verifier Observability Tool success, mission failure Verifier Observability Surface-level drift Archivist Memory, Ownership Coupled urgent-fix bundles ship together Dispatcher, Runtime Ownership Idempotent secret bootstrap Runtime Ownership Render the desired state Runtime Memory, Ownership Ungoverned secret mutation Runtime Ownership, Memory Stale hand-off Runtime Memory, Ownership Single-owner skills and docs Archivist Ownership, Memory Drive forward without permission stalls Dispatcher Observability, Fallbacks Post-mission review as ritual All Observability Decisions without landing rituals Dispatcher Ownership

docs.title: 6. Discussion
docs.text:
The taxonomy suggests that reliability in a multi-agent fleet comes from designing roles before designing agents. A common mistake is to start with a set of model instances and then decide what each should do. A more robust approach is to define the responsibilities, the contracts between them, and the fallbacks, and only then map roles to runtime instances.

Example. A team starts with three generic agent instances and assigns tasks ad hoc. Soon the same manifest is edited by two agents, a secret is rotated by a third, and no one knows who should verify the result. Applying the taxonomy first would have assigned ownership of the manifest to the Builder, the secret to the Runtime, and the verification gate to the Verifier.

The six roles here are not claimed to be exhaustive. Other fleets may need a human-facing liaison, a security role, or a compliance role. The taxonomy is offered as a starting vocabulary and as a way to make coordination assumptions explicit.

docs.title: 7. Limitations and future work
docs.text: This taxonomy is derived from one fleet. Other systems may organise responsibilities differently. Future work could compare this taxonomy to AutoGen, CrewAI, and DSPy roles, and could evaluate whether adding or splitting roles improves outcomes. I pursue an evaluation protocol for persistent multi-agent workflows in a companion paper.

docs.title: 8. Conclusion
docs.text: I proposed a role-oriented taxonomy with six responsibilities—Dispatcher, Builder, Runtime, Verifier, Researcher, and Archivist—and four cross-cutting concerns: memory, ownership, observability, and fallbacks. Mapping companion field-study patterns onto this taxonomy shows that most failures and most effective strategies concern the contracts between roles rather than the capability of any single agent. I offer this taxonomy as a design tool for practitioners and as groundwork for comparative research in multi-agent coordination.

docs.text: See bibliography.

docs.title: Memory-Centric Collaboration: A Position on Scaling Agent Fleets
docs.text: Abstract. I argue that scaling long-running multi-agent systems depends on shared persistent state at least as much as on orchestration logic alone. The dominant mental model treats agents as discrete reasoning units coordinated by a central dispatcher. I contrast this with a memory-centric model in which agents share an externalised record of identity, goals, events, and decisions. Drawing on operational experience with a six-role agent fleet, I show that the most costly failures were caused by implicit or missing memory contracts, while the most durable strategies made those contracts explicit. I outline design principles for memory-centric collaboration and respond to common objections.

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
The prevailing way to design multi-agent systems is orchestration-centric. One agent, often called a planner or manager, decides which agent should act next. The emphasis is on routing, delegation, and conversation flow. This model works well for short tasks with clear goals and fresh context. It works less well when agents operate for weeks, recover from restarts, or inherit responsibilities started by another instance.

I operated a fleet organised around six roles over an extended period. The failures that consumed the most time were rarely failures of reasoning. They were failures of shared state: a session defaulting to the wrong identity, a credential rotated without telling downstream agents, a long thread summarised until a constraint disappeared, a decision approved but never recorded as a landing plan. In each case, the orchestration logic was doing what it was told; the missing element was memory.

This paper makes a simple but consequential claim: in long-running agent systems, shared persistent state is at least as important as orchestration. I call this the memory-centric view.

docs.title: 2. The orchestration-centric view and its limits
docs.text:
In the orchestration-centric view, the central problem is: given a task, which agent should do what, in what order? The solution is a dispatcher, a workflow graph, or a conversation loop. Memory, if it exists, is treated as an optional cache for convenience.

This view has produced useful systems. It also has predictable limits:

  • Identity is implicit. A session resolver may store state in-process, so a restarted agent defaults to a generic identity and forgets the active task.

Example. A user asks the fleet to refactor a configuration store. The conversation spans ten turns, then the agent process restarts. Because session state is in-process, the new instance defaults to a generic identity and asks the user to restate the goal. The original thread is lost.

  • Goals are mutable. A long conversation is summarised to save context, and the summary silently drops a constraint.

Example. A twelve-turn thread comparing deployment strategies is summarised to fit the context window. The summary drops the constraint that the service must stay available. The next agent proposes a strategy that requires a brief outage. The constraint was not rejected; it was compressed away.

  • Changes are invisible. One agent writes to a different namespace or rotates a secret; other agents fail hours later with no obvious cause.

Example. A Runtime role rotates a credential used by the Archivist to write to the shared memory service. The rotation succeeds locally, but no event is broadcast. Hours later, memory lookups fail with authentication errors, and the symptom is first diagnosed as amnesia in unrelated tasks.

  • Decisions evaporate. The fleet agrees on a fix but records no merge, deploy, verification, or rollback plan, so approved work rots.

Example. The fleet agrees a critical fix is needed. The Builder writes the patch, but no landing plan is recorded. The Runtime role assumes the Builder will merge; the Builder assumes the Runtime role will deploy. Two weeks later the patch is still on a branch and production is still broken.

These are all failures of memory in the broad sense: the system cannot remember who it is, what it decided, what changed, or what it still owes.

docs.title: 3. What memory-centric collaboration means
docs.text:
A memory-centric model treats shared state as a first-class design primitive, not as a caching layer. The shared record contains at least:

  • Identity. Who is acting and on whose behalf.
  • Goal artefacts. Immutable or versioned records of the original goal and current phase.
  • Event log. A chronological, append-only record of changes made by any role.
  • Contracts. Ownership, deliverables, verification gates, and landing plans.
  • Local state. Per-agent drafts, preferences, and transient scaffolding that is not shared until promoted.

In this model, orchestration still matters, but it operates on top of the shared record. The Dispatcher’s job is not merely to route; it is to keep the shared record consistent with reality.

Example. The Dispatcher advances a migration only after the Verifier posts a green verdict in the shared record. The verdict is not a private message; it is an artefact that other roles can query. If the Dispatcher restarts, it resumes from the shared record rather than from conversation context.

docs.title: 4. Evidence from the field study
docs.text:
The companion field study catalogue contains several patterns that illustrate the memory-centric view:

  • Externalise session state. Session affinity survives restarts because session metadata lives outside any single process.
  • Explicit identity in shared tools. Memory writes require caller and subject identity; defaults are forbidden.
  • Local-first, shared-when-useful. Only stable, source-attributed facts are promoted to the shared store.
  • Single-owner skills and docs. Guidance has a canonical owner and location, preventing conflicting copies from becoming the de facto memory.
  • Gate before forward motion. A Verifier posts a green/red verdict that becomes part of the shared record before the Dispatcher advances.
  • Render the desired state. Declarative tools always emit the manifest, so the shared record matches the desired state.

Each of these is a memory contract. Each prevents a failure that orchestration alone could not catch.

docs.title: 5. Design principles
docs.text:
From the field-study patterns I derive the following principles for memory-centric collaboration:

  1. Externalise before scaling. Do not promise horizontal scale or recovery until session state, identity, and goal artefacts live outside individual processes.
  2. Make identity explicit. Every write to shared memory must carry caller and subject identity. Reject ambiguous calls rather than silently default.
  3. Version or freeze goals. The original goal and current phase should be recoverable even after summarisation or restart.
  4. Append events, not just state. A change log makes causality visible and debugging tractable.
  5. Separate local from shared. Transient scaffolding belongs to the agent; cross-agent facts belong to the shared store.
  6. Treat decisions as artefacts. Every approved change should produce a merge plan, deploy plan, verification gate, and rollback plan in the shared record.
  7. Make contracts observable. The fleet should be able to query who owns what, what is blocked, and what has been verified.

Example. A secret rotation is approved. The shared record contains: the Runtime role owns rotation, the Archivist owns re-seeding, the Verifier owns the post-rotation health gate, and a rollback plan is attached. Any role can query the record to see the current phase and who is blocked.

docs.title: 6. Counterarguments and responses
docs.text:
“Centralised memory creates a single point of failure.”

It does, which is why local-first, in-memory fallbacks, and event logs are part of the model. The goal is not to replace local state but to define what must be shared and what can be recovered.

Example. If the shared memory service is unavailable, agents fall back to local state and append events to a local log. When the service returns, the local events are replayed into the shared record. The system degrades gracefully rather than forgets.

“Explicit contracts slow the fleet down.”

They slow some tasks down, particularly when the fleet is learning a new convention. They speed up recovery, debugging, and coordination in steady state. The net effect depends on task duration and failure cost; in long-running operations, the balance favours contracts.

“This is just good software engineering, not a new AI insight.”

That is partly true. Many of these ideas come from SRE, databases, and distributed systems. The new part is applying them to agents whose context is bounded, whose identities are easy to lose, and whose actions are generated rather than programmed. The orchestration-centric view dominates current agent tooling; the memory-centric view is an alternative design centre.

docs.title: 7. Implications for tool builders
docs.text:
A memory-centric stance has implications for frameworks and platforms:

  • Provide shared memory as a primitive, not an afterthought.
  • Require identity on every read and write; do not make generic buckets the default.
  • Expose an append-only event log that agents and operators can query.
  • Support promotion workflows from local to shared state.
  • Treat skills, runbooks, and examples as shared memory with owners and version history.

Example. A shared-memory tool should reject a write unless both caller identity and subject identity are supplied. A generic default bucket should be opt-in, not the default, because it hides misrouting in multi-agent systems.

docs.title: 8. Conclusion
docs.text: I argued that scaling long-running multi-agent systems depends on shared persistent state at least as much as on orchestration. The memory-centric model makes identity, goals, events, and contracts first-class, externalised primitives. The patterns observed in a six-role operations fleet support this view: the most durable strategies all make memory contracts explicit, while the most damaging failures occurred when those contracts were implicit or missing. I offer this position as a design heuristic for practitioners and as a research question for the agent-systems community.

docs.text: See bibliography.

docs.title: Toward an Evaluation Protocol for Persistent Multi-Agent Workflows
docs.text: Abstract. Existing benchmarks for LLM agents focus on single-turn reasoning, tool use, or short cooperative tasks. They do not capture the coordination behaviours that matter in long-running operations fleets: role fidelity, memory correctness, identity preservation, and recovery after restart or scale. I propose a lightweight evaluation protocol for persistent multi-agent workflows. The protocol defines tasks that exercise the patterns observed in a companion field study, metrics for each task, and a minimal procedure for running and scoring them. The goal is to make multi-agent coordination measurable, comparable, and improvable.

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text:
For single models, the research community has broad coverage of reasoning, coding, and knowledge. For multi-agent systems, promising frameworks exist, but rigorous evaluations are scarcer.

I operated a six-role operations fleet over an extended period. The failures that mattered most were not solved by a better single model; they were solved by clearer contracts between roles. This paper asks how those contracts can be evaluated.

docs.title: 2. Related work
docs.text:
• SWE-bench evaluates whether a model can resolve real-world GitHub issues end to end. It is strong on software-engineering outcomes but does not test multi-agent coordination or persistence.
  • AgentBench evaluates LLMs as agents across multiple environments. It measures task success but rarely role fidelity or memory correctness across sessions.
  • MT-bench and chatbot arena focus on response quality. They do not evaluate whether an agent fleet remembers, owns, or lands work.

The gap is not a lack of tasks but a lack of tasks designed for long-running, multi-agent, memory-dependent workflows.

docs.title: 3. Evaluation requirements
docs.text:
From operational experience I derive five requirements for an evaluation protocol:

  1. Role fidelity. Does the right role act on the right task? Can a role decline work outside its domain?
  2. Memory correctness. After a restart or context switch, does the agent recover the original goal, identity, and constraints?
  3. Identity preservation. Can the fleet distinguish agents and roles in shared memory, or do writes collide?
  4. Recovery after restart/scale. Can the system resume a long-running workflow after process replacement or horizontal scaling?
  5. End-to-end outcome correctness. Does the composed workflow produce a correct result, not just correct intermediate steps?

Example. A fleet is asked to refactor a configuration store. Role fidelity means the Builder, not the Runtime, owns the code change. Memory correctness means that after a restart the new agent instance still knows the original goal was a refactor, not a deletion. End-to-end correctness means the store still works after the change.

docs.title: 4. Proposed tasks
docs.text: Each task is designed to exercise one or more requirements. They are stylised and anonymised; they do not depend on any specific system.

docs.title: Task 1: Role fidelity under ambiguity
docs.text:
A Dispatcher receives a vague request: "something is wrong with the service." The correct response is to route to the Runtime role for health diagnosis, not to the Builder for code changes. The task is scored by whether the first routed role is appropriate and whether the specialist can decline a misrouted task.

Example. The Dispatcher observes a slow endpoint and assumes a recent configuration change is at fault. It sends the Builder a request to revert the change. The correct behaviour is for the Builder to decline and escalate to the Runtime role, because the symptom points to infrastructure, not code.

docs.title: Task 2: Verifiable dispatch
docs.text:
A Dispatcher is given a task and must produce a dispatch message. A correct dispatch includes a concrete deliverable and a verification criterion. The task is scored by whether a downstream Verifier can determine green/red from the dispatch alone.

Example. A correct dispatch might read: "Update the health-check endpoint so the liveness probe returns HTTP 200 within 2 seconds; mark complete only when the Verifier reports green on staging." An incorrect dispatch reads: "Look into the health check."

docs.title: Task 3: Memory recovery after restart
docs.text:
A conversation is interrupted by a simulated process restart. The agent must resume from externalised session state and continue the original task without asking the user to restate the goal. The task is scored by goal recall and identity consistency.

Example. Mid-refactor, the agent process restarts. The resumed instance should retrieve the original goal and current phase from the shared memory service and continue, rather than ask, "What would you like me to do?"

docs.title: Task 4: Identity isolation in shared memory
docs.text:
Two agents write to a shared memory tool. One omits identity fields. The evaluation checks whether the memory tool rejects the write or whether the omission causes silent misrouting. The task is scored by correctness of the tool’s validation contract.

Example. Agent A writes a memory with explicit observer and subject identity. Agent B writes a memory with the subject identity omitted. The shared-memory tool should reject Agent B’s write, not silently place it in a generic bucket where other agents will miss it.

docs.title: Task 5: Silent dependency detection
docs.text:
An agent changes a shared config value. A downstream agent later fails. The evaluation checks whether the fleet can trace the failure back to the change through an event log or ownership record. The task is scored by causal tracing accuracy.

Example. A Runtime role changes a shared credential. A Researcher role fails hours later when querying the memory service. The fleet must be able to follow the event log from the Researcher failure back to the credential change and identify the Runtime role as the owner of that change.

docs.title: Task 6: End-to-end landing
docs.text:
A change is approved in conversation. The fleet must produce a merge plan, deploy plan, verification gate, and rollback plan in the shared record. The task is scored by completeness of the landing ritual and by whether the change actually reaches the simulated target environment.

Example. The fleet approves adding a new health check. The Dispatcher must ensure the Builder produces a patch, the Runtime role produces a deploy plan, the Verifier defines a green/red gate, and the Runtime role produces a rollback plan. The task is incomplete if any of these artefacts is missing.

docs.title: Task 7: Metric calibration
docs.text:
A benchmark scoring function is provided that collapses successful outcomes to the same score. The agent or operator must recalibrate the function so that faster and slower successes are distinguishable. The task is scored by whether the recalibrated metric reflects task quality.

Example. The initial scoring function gives a slow but correct agent the same score as an agent that produced no output. A correct recalibration rewards correctness first and adds a secondary efficiency score that does not erase the correctness distinction.

docs.title: 5. Metrics
docs.text:
For each task I propose the following metrics:

  • Outcome accuracy. Did the workflow reach the correct end state?
  • Role accuracy. Was the task routed to the correct role?
  • Memory fidelity. After restart, how much of the original goal and identity was preserved?
  • Contract coverage. How many required artefacts (deliverable, verification, landing plan, rollback plan) were produced?
  • Causal traceability. How many steps were needed to trace a failure to its source?
  • Metric informativeness. Does the scoring function distinguish successful outcomes by task quality rather than collapsing them?

Metrics should be reported per task and, where possible, per pattern. A single aggregate score is less useful than a profile across the requirements.

docs.title: 6. Procedure
docs.text:
A minimal run of the protocol would follow these steps:

  1. Select tasks. Choose the tasks that match the target fleet’s responsibilities.
  2. Provide a shared environment. Give the agents a memory service, event log, and tool sandbox.
  3. Inject incidents. Seed each task with the failure condition it is designed to exercise.
  4. Run the agents. Allow the fleet to coordinate without human prompting except for true blockers.
  5. Score. Apply the metrics above, preferably with an automated Verifier role or a deterministic oracle.
  6. Report a profile. Report scores per task and per requirement, not just a single number.

Example. A pilot run might report: role fidelity 0.85, memory recovery 0.40, identity isolation 0.70, end-to-end landing 0.30, metric informativeness 0.90. The profile immediately shows that memory and landing are the weak points, even if the fleet scores well on routing and metrics.

docs.title: 7. Pilot expectations
docs.text: I expect a pilot run to reveal that current multi-agent systems struggle most with memory correctness and end-to-end landing. Single-turn tool use is likely to score well; role fidelity under ambiguity and silent-dependency tracing are likely to score poorly. The value of the protocol is to make those weaknesses visible and comparable across systems.

docs.title: 8. Limitations and future work
docs.text: This protocol is a proposal, not an established benchmark. The tasks are stylised and may need adaptation to real systems. Future work includes building reference implementations, collecting human baselines, and agreeing on common task definitions. I hope the companion field study and patterns catalogue provide the empirical grounding for that work.

docs.title: 9. Conclusion
docs.text: I proposed an evaluation protocol for persistent multi-agent workflows. The protocol focuses on role fidelity, memory correctness, identity preservation, recovery, and end-to-end outcomes. By designing tasks around the patterns observed in a long-running operations fleet, the protocol aims to measure the coordination behaviours that matter most in practice. I offer it to the evaluation community as a starting point for making agent fleets comparable and improvable.

docs.text: See bibliography.
